title: Agentic AI Reference Architecture
subtitle: A technology reference architecture for the Agentic Organisation, synthesised from 30+ industry frameworks.
description: Nine technology layers and three cross-cutting concerns that define how enterprises build, deploy, and govern AI agent systems at scale.

layers.label: Experience & Applications
layers.short: Chat UIs, co-pilots, agent teammates, APIs, voice
layers.description: The interface layer where humans and systems interact with AI agents. Includes conversational UIs, embedded co-pilots, autonomous agent teammates, programmatic APIs, and voice interfaces. The experience layer must support streaming responses, multi-turn context, and graceful degradation when agents are unavailable.

components.name: Conversational UIs
components.description: Natural language chat interfaces with multi-turn dialogue, context management, and citation rendering.

components.name: Co-Pilots
components.description: AI assistants embedded in existing applications — IDEs, office suites, analytics tools — augmenting human workflows in-place.

components.name: Agent Teammates
components.description: Autonomous agents operating as virtual team members, executing tasks independently with human oversight and escalation.

components.name: APIs & Programmatic Access
components.description: REST, GraphQL, and streaming endpoints for system-to-agent and agent-to-system communication at machine speed.

components.name: Voice & Multimodal
components.description: Speech-to-text and text-to-speech pipelines, image understanding, and multimodal input/output for agent interaction.

implementation.when: Building conversational agent interfaces with real-time token streaming
implementation.description: Use the AI SDK's useChat hook or AG-UI protocol for standardised streaming. SSE provides the transport layer, with progressive rendering of agent responses and inline citations.

implementation.when: Augmenting existing applications rather than building standalone agent UIs
implementation.description: Embed agent capabilities directly into the tools people already use. Co-pilots reduce context switching and adoption friction compared to standalone chat interfaces.

implementation.when: System-to-agent communication without human UI
implementation.description: Expose agent capabilities as versioned REST APIs with OpenAPI specs. Enables programmatic access from CI/CD pipelines, other agents, and enterprise automation.

layers.label: Guardrails & Safety
layers.short: Input/output validation, PII redaction, content policy, schema enforcement
layers.description: The safety layer that validates every agent input and output before it reaches users or downstream systems. Prevents hallucination propagation, enforces content policies, redacts sensitive data, and ensures outputs conform to expected schemas. Operates as an inline filter, not a post-hoc check.

components.name: Input Validation
components.description: Prompt injection detection, jailbreak filters, and input sanitisation before agent processing.

components.name: Output Enforcement
components.description: JSON schema validation, format compliance, and structured output guarantees for downstream consumers.

components.name: Content Policy
components.description: Topic restrictions, toxicity filters, brand-safety rules, and regulatory content constraints enforced at runtime.

components.name: PII Redaction
components.description: Automated detection and masking of personally identifiable information in both inputs and outputs.

components.name: Hallucination Detection
components.description: Grounding checks, citation verification, and confidence scoring to flag unsubstantiated claims.

implementation.when: Need configurable input/output validation without custom code
implementation.description: Declarative guardrail definitions that validate agent inputs and outputs against configurable policies. Chain multiple validators (PII, toxicity, topic) in a pipeline before and after agent execution.

implementation.when: Agent outputs must conform to strict schemas for downstream systems
implementation.description: Constrain LLM outputs to valid JSON schemas at generation time rather than post-hoc validation. Instructor wraps API calls with Pydantic models; Outlines uses constrained decoding for guaranteed schema conformance.

implementation.when: Hallucination risk is high and outputs must be traceable to sources
implementation.description: Validate that agent claims are grounded in retrieved context. RAGAS provides faithfulness and relevance metrics. Vectara's HHEM scores hallucination probability. Combine with mandatory citation rendering in the experience layer.

layers.label: Orchestration & Planning
layers.short: Task decomposition, multi-agent coordination, workflow engines, state management
layers.description: The coordination layer that decomposes complex goals into executable tasks, routes them to the right agents, and manages state across multi-step workflows. Supports sequential, parallel, hierarchical, and swarm orchestration patterns. The critical difference from traditional workflow engines: orchestration must handle non-deterministic agent outputs and dynamic re-planning.

components.name: Task Decomposition
components.description: Breaking complex objectives into sub-tasks with dependency graphs, priority ordering, and resource estimation.

components.name: Multi-Agent Coordination
components.description: Routing tasks across specialised agents — sequential handoffs, fan-out/fan-in, group chat, and supervisor patterns.

components.name: Workflow Engines
components.description: Durable execution engines (LangGraph, Temporal, Step Functions) with retry logic, checkpointing, and failover.

components.name: State Management
components.description: Maintaining conversation context, task progress, and shared state across agents within and across sessions.

components.name: Human-in-the-Loop
components.description: Escalation paths, approval gates, and confidence-threshold routing that bring humans into agent workflows at decision points.

implementation.when: Complex multi-step workflows with branching logic and error recovery
implementation.description: Model agent workflows as directed acyclic graphs with conditional edges. LangGraph provides Python-native DAGs with state management. Temporal adds durable execution with automatic retries and human-in-the-loop wait states.

implementation.when: Multiple specialised agents collaborating on a shared objective
implementation.description: Define agents by role (Researcher, Analyst, Writer) with specific tools and models. CrewAI provides structured crew definitions; AutoGen offers flexible conversation patterns. Both support hierarchical and flat team structures.

implementation.when: Central coordination needed with dynamic agent selection
implementation.description: A supervisor agent decomposes tasks, selects the best sub-agent for each step, and synthesises results. Works well when the set of available agents changes or when tasks require dynamic planning.

layers.label: Tools & Integration
layers.short: Function calling, MCP servers, API connectors, enterprise systems
layers.description: The capability layer that connects agents to the outside world — enterprise systems, databases, APIs, and external services. Agents are only as capable as their available tools. This layer manages tool registration, discovery, invocation, and access control. MCP (Model Context Protocol) is emerging as the standard for tool exposure.

components.name: Function Calling
components.description: Structured tool invocation via JSON Schema definitions — the primary mechanism for agent-to-tool communication.

components.name: MCP Servers
components.description: Model Context Protocol servers that expose enterprise tools, resources, and prompts to any MCP-compatible agent.

components.name: API Connectors
components.description: Pre-built integrations for CRM (Salesforce), ERP (SAP), HR (Workday), ITSM (ServiceNow), and other enterprise systems.

components.name: Data Access
components.description: Database query tools, file system access, knowledge base retrieval, and structured data extraction capabilities.

components.name: External Services
components.description: Web search, code execution, email, calendar, and third-party SaaS integrations available to agents.

implementation.when: Exposing enterprise tools to any MCP-compatible agent
implementation.description: Build MCP servers that wrap existing APIs, databases, and services. Each server exposes tools (callable functions), resources (readable data), and prompts (reusable templates). The agent discovers and invokes tools via the standardised MCP protocol.

implementation.when: Centralised access control and rate limiting for agent tool calls
implementation.description: Route all agent-to-tool communication through an API gateway that enforces authentication, rate limits, and audit logging. Prevents agents from directly accessing backend systems and provides a single chokepoint for security policy.

implementation.when: Agent needs to discover and use tools not known at build time
implementation.description: Maintain a registry of available tools with OpenAPI-compatible schemas. Agents query the registry at runtime to discover tools matching their current task, enabling extensibility without redeployment.

layers.label: Agent Runtime & Memory
layers.short: Execution sandboxes, session memory, long-term knowledge, embedding stores
layers.description: The execution environment where agents run and persist knowledge. Manages agent lifecycle (create, execute, suspend, resume, terminate), provides isolated sandboxes for safe execution, and maintains both short-term session memory and long-term organisational knowledge. Memory is what separates a stateless API call from an intelligent agent.

components.name: Execution Sandboxes
components.description: Isolated runtime environments (containers, serverless functions) with resource limits, timeout policies, and security boundaries.

components.name: Session Memory
components.description: Short-term conversational context — what the agent knows about the current task, user, and interaction history.

components.name: Long-Term Knowledge
components.description: Persistent memory across sessions — learned preferences, past decisions, organisational knowledge, and accumulated expertise.

components.name: Vector / Embedding Stores
components.description: Semantic search infrastructure (Pinecone, Qdrant, Milvus) for similarity-based retrieval of relevant context and documents.

components.name: Agent Lifecycle Management
components.description: Creation, versioning, deployment, scaling, suspension, and retirement of agent instances across the organisation.

implementation.when: Enterprise deployment with minimal infrastructure management
implementation.description: Use cloud-managed runtimes that handle agent lifecycle, scaling, and monitoring. Trade customisation flexibility for operational simplicity. Best for organisations that want to focus on agent logic rather than infrastructure.

implementation.when: Full control over execution environment, sovereignty requirements
implementation.description: Run agents in self-managed containers with autoscaling. KEDA provides event-driven scaling based on queue depth or request rate. E2B offers secure code execution sandboxes for agents that generate and run code.

implementation.when: Agents need to remember context across sessions and learn from interactions
implementation.description: Layer short-term memory (Redis/in-memory for session context) with long-term memory (Mem0/Zep for cross-session knowledge). LangMem provides memory management as a LangGraph component. Critical for agents that build user models over time.

layers.label: Protocols & Interoperability
layers.description: The standards layer that enables agents from different vendors, frameworks, and organisations to communicate. Without interoperability protocols, every agent integration is bespoke. Four protocols are emerging as the foundation: MCP for tool access, A2A for agent-to-agent delegation, ACP for agent communication, and ANP for network-level agent interaction.

components.name: MCP (Model Context Protocol)
components.description: Anthropic-originated, now Linux Foundation. Standardises how agents access tools, resources, and prompts. 97M+ monthly SDK downloads.

components.name: A2A (Agent-to-Agent)
components.description: Google-led protocol for standardised agent delegation — discovery, handshake, task transfer, and result return between agents.

components.name: ACP (Agent Communication Protocol)
components.description: IBM-led protocol for structured agent-to-agent messaging with typed payloads and conversation management.

components.name: ANP (Agent Network Protocol)
components.description: Cisco-led protocol for agent interaction at the network layer — routing, discovery, and trust across organisational boundaries.

components.name: OpenAPI & JSON-RPC
components.description: Established API standards that underpin agent tool definitions and structured communication formats.

implementation.when: Primary need is connecting agents to enterprise tools and data
implementation.description: Start with MCP as the integration backbone. Build MCP servers for internal tools, use community servers for common services (GitHub, Slack, databases). SSE transport for remote servers, stdio for local.

implementation.when: Agents from different teams or vendors need to delegate tasks to each other
implementation.description: Use A2A when agents need to hand off work across organisational or vendor boundaries. Agent Cards advertise capabilities; the Task Protocol manages request lifecycle. Complements MCP — A2A is agent-to-agent, MCP is agent-to-tool.

implementation.when: Mixed protocol environment requiring translation between standards
implementation.description: When different parts of the organisation use different protocols (MCP, A2A, legacy REST), a gateway translates between them. Allows gradual migration without forcing all teams to adopt the same protocol simultaneously.

layers.label: Foundation Models
layers.short: LLMs, SLMs, domain models, model serving, model routing
layers.description: The intelligence layer providing reasoning capabilities. Not one model but a portfolio: large language models for complex reasoning, small language models for cost-efficient routine tasks, domain-specific models for specialised knowledge, and vision/speech models for multimodal capabilities. Model routing directs each request to the optimal model based on task complexity, cost, and latency requirements.

components.name: Large Language Models
components.description: General-purpose reasoning engines (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) for complex analysis, planning, and creative tasks.

components.name: Small Language Models
components.description: Cost-efficient models (Phi, Mistral, Llama) for routine classification, extraction, and structured output tasks.

components.name: Domain-Specific Models
components.description: Fine-tuned or specialised models for finance, legal, medical, and other regulated domains requiring deep expertise.

components.name: Model Serving
components.description: Infrastructure for hosting and scaling model inference — vLLM, TensorRT, managed endpoints — with latency and throughput SLAs.

components.name: Model Routing
components.description: Intelligent request routing that selects the optimal model per task based on complexity, cost, latency, and capability requirements.

implementation.when: Multiple models needed with different cost, latency, and capability profiles
implementation.description: Route each request to the optimal model based on task complexity. Use frontier models (Claude, GPT-4) for complex reasoning, SLMs (Phi, Mistral) for routine classification. LiteLLM provides a unified API across 100+ providers; OpenRouter adds automatic fallback.

implementation.when: Data sovereignty, cost control at scale, or offline operation required
implementation.description: Host open-weight models on own infrastructure. vLLM provides high-throughput serving with PagedAttention. Ollama simplifies local development. TensorRT-LLM optimises for NVIDIA GPUs. Essential for regulated industries where data cannot leave the organisation.

implementation.when: General models lack domain accuracy for specialised tasks
implementation.description: Fine-tune base models on domain-specific data (legal, medical, financial) to improve accuracy and reduce hallucination. Unsloth provides 2x faster fine-tuning with 60% less memory. Start with few-shot prompting; fine-tune only when prompting hits its limits.

layers.label: Data & Knowledge
layers.short: Data lakes, vector stores, knowledge graphs, RAG pipelines, semantic layer
layers.description: The knowledge foundation that gives agents access to organisational data, documents, and structured knowledge. Agents without data access are limited to their training knowledge. This layer manages data ingestion, transformation, semantic indexing, and retrieval — including RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines that ground agent responses in enterprise-specific facts.

components.name: Data Lakes & Warehouses
components.description: Centralised storage for structured and unstructured enterprise data — the raw material agents need to reason about the business.

components.name: Knowledge Graphs
components.description: Entity-relationship models that encode organisational knowledge — people, processes, systems, policies — in machine-traversable form.

components.name: RAG Pipelines
components.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation: document chunking, embedding, indexing, and retrieval to ground agent responses in source documents.

components.name: Semantic Layer
components.description: Business ontologies and concept hierarchies that give agents a shared vocabulary for the organisation's domain.

components.name: Data Ingestion & ETL
components.description: Connectors, transformers, and pipelines that keep agent-accessible data current — batch for stable data, streaming for volatile data.

implementation.when: Agents need to answer questions grounded in enterprise documents
implementation.description: Build retrieval-augmented generation pipelines: ingest documents, chunk semantically, embed, store in a vector database, retrieve relevant chunks at query time. LlamaIndex excels at complex document structures; Haystack offers production-grade pipelines.

implementation.when: Relationships between entities matter more than document content
implementation.description: Extract entities and relationships from enterprise data using LLMs, store in a graph database. Agents query the graph for structured reasoning — 'which teams depend on this system?' Complements RAG for relational questions.

implementation.when: Agents need a shared, consistent vocabulary for business concepts
implementation.description: Define business metrics, dimensions, and relationships in a semantic layer that agents query instead of raw data. Ensures consistency — 'revenue' means the same thing to every agent. dbt provides metric definitions; Cube adds a query API.

layers.label: Infrastructure & Compute
layers.short: GPU/CPU, Kubernetes, cloud platforms, networking, edge
layers.description: The physical and virtual compute foundation. AI workloads demand GPU clusters for model training and inference, container orchestration for agent scaling, and low-latency networking for real-time interactions. Cloud platforms provide managed services, but sovereignty requirements increasingly drive hybrid and edge deployments.

components.name: GPU / CPU Compute
components.description: Hardware accelerators (NVIDIA, AMD, custom ASICs) for model inference and training, with CPU for orchestration workloads.

components.name: Container Orchestration
components.description: Kubernetes-based platforms for scaling agent instances, managing resource allocation, and ensuring availability.

components.name: Cloud Platforms
components.description: Managed services from hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, GCP) providing model hosting, storage, networking, and security infrastructure.

components.name: Networking & Edge
components.description: Low-latency connectivity, edge compute for latency-sensitive agent interactions, and network segmentation for security.

components.name: CI/CD & DevOps
components.description: Automated build, test, and deployment pipelines adapted for AI workloads — model versioning, A/B testing, canary deployments.

implementation.when: Scaling model inference across multiple GPUs or cloud providers
implementation.description: Use Ray for distributed inference across GPU clusters. SkyPilot optimises cost by routing to the cheapest available cloud. Modal and RunPod provide serverless GPU compute for burst workloads without managing infrastructure.

implementation.when: Agent traffic varies significantly and infrastructure costs matter
implementation.description: Scale agent instances based on demand. KEDA triggers scaling from queue depth or HTTP request rate. Serverless options (Lambda, Cloud Run) provide zero-to-many scaling with pay-per-use. Combine with warm pools for latency-sensitive agents.

implementation.when: Need end-to-end visibility from infrastructure through agent behaviour
implementation.description: Instrument everything with OpenTelemetry from day one. Trace requests from load balancer through agent orchestration to model inference. Agent-specific spans should capture model, tokens, latency, and cost.

cross_cutting.label: Observability
cross_cutting.short: Tracing, cost dashboards, eval pipelines, audit trails
cross_cutting.description: End-to-end visibility into agent behaviour across all layers. Traditional APM is insufficient — agent observability must capture semantic quality (was the answer correct?), not just technical health (did the API return 200?). Includes distributed tracing, cost attribution, evaluation pipelines, and audit trails.

components.name: Distributed Tracing
components.description: OpenTelemetry-based tracing across agent chains — from user request through orchestration, tool calls, and model invocations.

components.name: Cost Attribution
components.description: Per-request, per-agent, per-model cost tracking with dashboards for budget management and optimisation.

components.name: Evaluation Pipelines
components.description: Automated quality assessment using golden datasets, A/B testing, and statistical metrics for agent output quality.

components.name: Audit Trails
components.description: Immutable logs of every agent decision, tool invocation, and output — required for compliance, debugging, and accountability.

cross_cutting.label: Governance
cross_cutting.short: Identity, RBAC, policy-as-code, agent registries, human-in-the-loop
cross_cutting.description: The control framework that ensures agents operate within organisational boundaries. Includes agent identity and authentication, role-based access control, policy-as-code enforcement, agent registries for inventory management, and human-in-the-loop approval workflows for high-stakes decisions.

components.name: Agent Identity
components.description: Cryptographically verifiable agent identities using OIDC/JWT — every agent action traceable to a registered identity.

components.name: Policy-as-Code
components.description: Machine-executable governance rules (OPA, Cedar) that enforce compliance at deployment speed, not review-cycle speed.

components.name: Agent Registry
components.description: Central catalogue of all deployed agents — capabilities, owners, dependencies, health status, and compliance posture.

components.name: Access Control
components.description: RBAC and ABAC for agent permissions — per-session auth, minimum privilege, time-limited tokens, tool-level whitelisting.

cross_cutting.label: Security
cross_cutting.short: Zero-trust, per-tool-call PEP, agent identity, environment isolation
cross_cutting.description: Zero-trust security applied to every agent interaction. Traditional perimeter security is insufficient — agents require per-tool-call policy enforcement points, isolated execution environments, and cryptographic identity verification. Covers new AI-specific attack vectors: prompt injection, model extraction, data poisoning, and training data leakage.

components.name: Zero-Trust Architecture
components.description: No implicit trust — every agent request authenticated, authorised, and validated regardless of network position or prior interactions.

components.name: Policy Enforcement Points
components.description: Inline validation at every tool call, API invocation, and data access — not just at the perimeter but at every interaction boundary.

components.name: Environment Isolation
components.description: Sandboxed execution with resource limits, network restrictions, and blast-radius containment for each agent instance.

components.name: AI-Specific Threat Protection
components.description: Defences against prompt injection, model extraction, data poisoning, adversarial inputs, and training data exfiltration.

reference_architectures.name: Salesforce
reference_architectures.description: Most comprehensive enterprise IT reference. Adds four new layers — Semantic, AI/ML, Agentic, and Enterprise Orchestration — to the traditional five-layer IT stack.

reference_architectures.name: Microsoft
reference_architectures.description: Protocol-first architecture centred on interoperability. Defines eight core services from communication protocols to evaluation and governance.

reference_architectures.name: Google Cloud
reference_architectures.description: Full-stack reference from frontend frameworks through agent development kits to model runtime, with A2A protocol for multi-agent systems.

reference_architectures.name: AWS
reference_architectures.description: Managed services approach with Bedrock at the centre. Supervisor and Broker orchestration patterns for multi-agent coordination.

reference_architectures.name: Forrester
reference_architectures.description: Governance-first perspective with the Agent Control Plane providing out-of-band oversight across heterogeneous agent estates.

reference_architectures.name: McKinsey
reference_architectures.framework: Five-Pillar Agentic Organisation + Agentic AI Mesh
reference_architectures.description: Whole-enterprise model covering business model, operating model, governance, workforce, and technology. The Agentic AI Mesh provides the connective orchestration layer.

reference_architectures.name: Bain
reference_architectures.description: Foundation-up approach emphasising modernised core platforms, interoperability (MCP standard), and distributed accountability across domain teams.

reference_architectures.name: AIMultiple
reference_architectures.description: Technology-agnostic layer model with competitive moat analysis per layer. Cleanest mapping of the full technology stack from infrastructure to applications.
